coredata.dc:title: Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots for wavelength-selective and ultrasensitive Cu2+ sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Daqin
Xu, Min
Wu, Weiwei
Li, Shichen
coredata.dc:description: Water dispersible carbon dots with multi-color visible luminescence were fabricated by a facile solvothermal route using citric acid/urea as carbon/nitrogen sources and dimethylformamide as solvent. Importantly, the intense long-wavelength red emission from carbon dots was selectively quenched upon the addition of Cu2+ ions, making them promising application as Cu2+ sensing material. There was no significant interference by other metal ions for Cu2+ detecting and the detection limit reached as low as 40 nM. Such sensing ability was almost reversible without any remarkable effect on sensitivity after introducing strong Cu2+ chelator ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid. It is expected that the investigated carbon dots with strong visible absorption and red emission as well as ultrasensitive Cu2+ sensing may find practical applications in biodetecting and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical materials
Carbon dots
Luminescence
Cu2+ sensing
Energy transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM micrograph of C-dots. Inset is HRTEM image of an individual C-dot. (b) Raman spectrum of C-dots under the excitation of 633 nm laser. (c) XPS full survey and (d) high-resolution XPS of N1s spectrum for C-dots.

(a) Excitation-emission mapping of C-dot sample and (b) the corresponding excitation-wavelength-dependent PL spectra. (c) Contour plots of time-resolved data for C-dots dispersed in aqueous solution. Inset of (c) shows decay curves of C-dots by monitoring typical blue (450 nm), yellow (550 nm) and red (650 nm) emissions, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Absorption and blue/yellow/red emission spectra of C-dots under the excitation of different wavelength lights. (b–f) Excitation-wavelength-dependent PL spectra with increase of C-dot content in aqueous solution. Insets of (f) show the corresponding fluorescent photographs of high-content C-dot dispersed solution under the irradiation of UV (365 nm, left) and blue (465 nm, right) lights, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
PL spectra of C-dots dispersed in solutions with and without the addition of Cu2+ ions (50 μM) under the excitation of different wavelength lights: (a) 360 nm, (b) 430 nm and (c) 550 nm.

(a–c) PL intensity ratios (I/I0) for C-dots dispersed in solutions with and without the addition of metal ions under the excitation of different wavelength lights (360 nm, 430 nm and 550 nm). The concentration of metal ions is fixed to 50 μM. (d) Cu2+ concentration-dependent red PL intensity ratio (I/I0) for C-dots dispersed in Cu2+ aqueous solution. (e) Stern-Volmer plot as a function of Cu2+ concentration. (f) Selective PL response for C-dots dispersed in Cu2+ (50 μM) aqueous solution with the interference of 50 μM other metal ions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) PL spectra of C-dots dispersed in (1) aqueous solution, (2) Cu2+ aqueous solution, (3) Cu2+/EDTA aqueous solution under the excitation of 550 nm light. (b) Quenching and recovery of C-dot emission intensity up to seven cycles. (c) Photographs of the corresponding C-dot solutions.

(a) Schematic structure model of the present C-dots with pyrrolic N on the surface. (b) Proposed yellow light excitation (Ex) and red emission (Em) as well as energy transfer quenching mechanism for C-dot solution with the addition of Cu2+ ions. Fluorescence decay curves of C-dot solution (c) with the addition of different metal ions and (d) with increase of Cu2+ concentration. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS elemental analysis and QY measurement results for C-dots.

head.ce:title: Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots for wavelength-selective and ultrasensitive Cu2+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials & Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Nonferrous Materials Science and Engineering, Education Ministry, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Nonferrous Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083 PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water dispersible carbon dots with multi-color visible luminescence were fabricated by a facile solvothermal route using citric acid/urea as carbon/nitrogen sources and dimethylformamide as solvent. Importantly, the intense long-wavelength red emission from carbon dots was selectively quenched upon the addition of Cu2+ ions, making them promising application as Cu2+ sensing material. There was no significant interference by other metal ions for Cu2+ detecting and the detection limit reached as low as 40 nM. Such sensing ability was almost reversible without any remarkable effect on sensitivity after introducing strong Cu2+ chelator ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid. It is expected that the investigated carbon dots with strong visible absorption and red emission as well as ultrasensitive Cu2+ sensing may find practical applications in biodetecting and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, detection of heavy metal ions, such as Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+, has attracted great attention because of its promising applications in environmental and biologic fields [1–7]. Particularly, copper ion is one of the most important trace elements in all living organisms and is vital to their health. Both Cu2+ excessive intake and Cu2+ deficiency could lead to serious diseases and cause dysfunction of biology [8,9]. Additionally, copper is one of the most abundant pollutants in drinking water. As a consequence, it is highly desirable to develop a facile, reusable and robust sensor with high selectivity and sensitivity for Cu2+ detecting.

In fact, fluorescence-based techniques have been demonstrated to be suitable for sensing diverse heavy metal ions in solutions and in living cells [10,11]. So far, a large amount of fluorescent probes, including organic molecules, quantum dots, noble metal nanoparticles and lanthanide fluophors, have been successfully explored [12–16]. However, the requirement of surface modification, low photostability for organic dye, usage of toxic elements for quantum dots as well as expensive cost for noble metals and lanthanide dopants usually limited their extensive applications. Alternatively, fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots), exhibiting superior optical performance with facile and cost-effective preparation, high quantum efficiency, water solubility, high photostability and excellent biocompatibility, have emerged and triggered increasing interest as probes for Cu2+ sensing [6,17–20]. Unfortunately, most reported C-dots for the detection of Cu2+ show blue-yellow luminescence with weak absorption in visible spectral region, which have an intrinsic disadvantage as the Cu2+ detecting probes since the biological tissues exhibit strong absorption for these blue-yellow visible emissions. Therefore, exploring C-dots with red and even near-infrared fluorescence falling within the “optical window” of biotissues could afford the deep tissue penetration. However, the investigations on efficient long-wavelength (orange and red) fluorescent C-dots for Cu2+ sensing are scarce so far.

Herein, we report a solvothermal synthesis of 2–3 nm water dispersible C-dots with efficient multi-color emissions. Structural and spectroscopic characterizations reveal that the long-wavelength red luminescence of C-dots is related to the pyrrolic N-based emitting states on the surface of particles. Cu2+ ions selectively bind to these N functional groups, being beneficial for wavelength-selective and ultrasensitive detection of Cu2+. The sensing concentration of Cu2+ is determined to be down to 40 nM via the quenching of red luminescence of C-dots, making them promising application for Cu2+ sensing in biofield.

All the raw chemicals, including N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), citric acid (CA), urea, petroleum ether, ethyl acetate, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), Ba(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, KCl, MnCl2, CuCl2, AgNO3, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, Mg(OAc)2 and Zn(OAc)2, were purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, P. R. China). All the materials were used as received without further refinement. Deionized water was used throughout this investigation.

C-dots were fabricated by a simple solvothermal technique as previously reported [21]. In a typical synthesis, citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were added into DMF solution (10 mL). Then the mixture was sealed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for a duration of 6 h. After introducing plenty of mixed petroleum ether and ethyl acetate solvent into reaction solution, C-dots were collected by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 30 min. The C-dots thus obtained can be easily dispersed in diverse solvent solutions, such as DMF, ethanol, methanol and water, for further characterization and use.

Stock solutions of the metal ions with a concentration of 1–200 μM and a dilute dispersion of c-dots (0.5 mg in 10 mL of H2O) were prepared in the deionized water. Photoluminescence (PL) experiments were carried out by placing a dispersion of metal ion solution (2.4 mL) in a quartz cuvette of 1 cm optical path length and then introducing the C-dots solution (0.1 mL) with the help of a micropipette. All the PL spectra were recorded after reaction for 1 min at room temperature. To recover the luminescence of C-dots, a solution of EDTA with a concentration of 50 mM was prepared and added successively (200 μL) to the mixture solution of C-dots and metal ions and maintained for 1 min. Almost up to 85% of the initial PL intensity of C-dots can be regained after the addition of EDTA solution.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2010) operated at 200 kV accelerating voltage was carried out to investigate microstructure of C-dots. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to detect the actual compositions of C-dots using VG Scientific ESCA Lab Mark II spectrometer equipped with two ultra-high vacuum 6 (UHV) chambers. Raman spectra were recorded using a RenishawinVia confocal Raman Microscope under the excitation of 633 nm laser. UV–Vis-IR spectrometer (Lambda 900) was used to measure absorption spectra. PL and PL excitation (PLE) spectra were recorded in the Edinburgh Instruments (EI) FS5 spectrofluorometer equipped with a continuous (150 W) lamp. PL lifetime measurements of C-dots were performed on a fluorescent lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, LifeSpec-II) based on a time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique under the excitation of 375 nm and 475 nm ps lasers. Quantum yield (QY), defined as the ratio of the emitted photons to the absorbed photons, is determined according to the following expression [22,23].
(1)
η =
n u m b e r
o f
p h o t o n s
e m i t t e d
n u m b e r
o f
p h o t o n s
a b s o r b e d
=
L
s a m p l e
E
r e f e r e n c e
−
E
s a m p l e
where η represents QY, L
sample the emission intensity, E
reference and E
sample the intensities of the excitation light not absorbed by the reference and the sample respectively. The measurements were carried out on a spectrofluorometer (FS5) equipped with an integrating sphere. All the experiments were performed at room temperature.

Two-dimensional fluorescent map (Fig. 2 a) demonstrates that the present C-dots show multi-band full-color emissions. Importantly, the luminescence shifting toward long-wavelength direction with increase of input excitation wavelength constitutes true color tuning since all the emissions exhibit comparable intensities (Fig. 2b). Lifetime is one of the intrinsic characteristics of an emitting-state. As a consequence, it is generally accepted that diverse emitting-states will exhibit different decay behaviors. To confirm the multi-color emissions originated from diverse emitting centers, time-resolved fluorescence was measured to evaluate the lifetimes of C-dots using time-correlated single-photon counting methodology, as revealed in Fig. 2c. Indeed, emission-wavelength-dependent decays are observed, and the mean decay lifetime gradually decreases from ∼8 ns to ∼1 ns with emission color changing from blue (450 nm) to red (650 nm), evidencing the multi-state fluorescent feature of the prepared C-dots. As tabulated in Table 1, quantum yields (QYs) of blue/yellow/red emissions upon the corresponding excitation of 360, 430 and 550 nm were evaluated to be about 41%, 43% and 39%, respectively. As a comparison, C-dots were also prepared without the addition of urea (N source), and only blue/yellow emissions were detected, confirming that the red fluorescence of the present C-dots is closely linked to the pyrrolic N on the surface of particles. Consequently, it is expected that blue, yellow and red emissions are originated from three diverse emitting states, i.e., sp2 C core, O- and N-related defect-states on the surfaces of C-dots, respectively.

Absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 a) of C-dots shows strong absorption in UV–visible spectral region extending to 600 nm. As the blue/yellow emissions fall in the absorption range of C-dots, it is expected the reabsorption phenomenon will be easily observed when the content of C-dots in aqueous solution is high enough. Indeed, as demonstrated in Fig. 3b–f, blue/yellow emission intensities gradually weaken with rise of C-dot content and pure red luminescence can be finally obtained. Evidently, red-emitting in the high-content C-dot dispersed solution under the irradiation of UV and blue lights is clearly observed (insets of Fig. 3f).

The abundant N-based functional groups on the surfaces of the prepared C-dots are expected to provide the feasibility as a nanosensing platform for the detection of Cu2+ ions because of the strong interactions between the functional groups and Cu2+ ions. Fig. 4 shows blue/yellow/red PL spectra of C-dots with and without the addition of Cu2+ ions under the excitation of 360 nm, 430 nm and 550 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the quenching of yellow/red PL from O-/N- related defect-states (Fig. 4b and c) is far more severe than the blue one from sp2 C emitting-state (Fig. 4a) since the chelation of Cu2+ with “N” and “O” related surface-states brings them into close proximity with each other and leads to energy transfer from yellow/red emitting states to Cu2+ quenching centers [6].

As shown in Fig. 5 a–c, a series of metal ions are introduced into the aqueous solution of C-dots and it is found that C-dots are very selective and sensitive to Cu2+ ions probably due to that Cu2+ ions have higher thermodynamic affinity with C-dots than other metal ions. Similarly, compared to the cases of blue/yellow luminescence, the quenching of red luminescence is significant. Subsequently, the N-related red PL intensity as a function of Cu2+ concentration was investigated to test the sensitivity of the C-dots for Cu2+ sensing. As shown in Fig. 5d, a gradual increase in the quenching of the red emission intensity of C-dots with increase of Cu2+ concentration was observed. In fact, the PL quenching data follow Stern-Volmer equation: I
0
/I = 1 + K [C], where K is Stern-Volmer constant, [C] is the Cu2+ concentration, I
0 and I are PL intensities of C-dots in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively [26]. A linear response for Cu2+ concentration in the range of 1–10 μM is achieved with a correlation coefficient (R
2 ) of 0.998 (Fig. 5e), confirming the excellent sensing property towards trace Cu2+ detection. The limit of detection (LOD) is determined to be ∼40 nM based on the formula of 3σ/K where σ is the standard deviation of the response and K (Stern-Volmer constant) equals to the slope of the calibration plot [6,26,27]. Worthy of notice, this LOD value is far lower than the sensitivity requirement of Cu2+ detection for drinkable water (20 μM) defined by US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [28]. Furthermore, the effect of interfering ions such as Ba2+, Ca2+, K+, Mn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mg2+ and Zn2+ on Cu2+ detection was investigated, as shown in Fig. 5f. It is found that there is no obvious influence of other metal ions on the present C-dot sensing system for Cu2+ detecting.

More importantly, introducing a strong Cu2+ chelator, such as EDTA, into the Cu2+ quenched C-dot solution will recover almost up to 85% of the initial PL intensity of C-dots (Fig. 6 a), and the whole process (i.e., addition of Cu2+ ions followed by EDTA) is recyclable for several times with only a slight decrease in the PL intensity (Fig. 6b), indicating that the detection of Cu2+ by using C-dots is completely reversible. In fact, the bright red color of C-dot aqueous solution can be easily regained after the addition of EDTA (Fig. 6c). Considering that long-wavelength excitation and emission are essentially required in biomedical image and detection, we believe that the investigated C-dots with strong multi-state visible absorption, intense red emission as well as ultrasensitive Cu2+ sensing may find practical application in bio-field after further optimizing their microstructure and optical performance.

Finally, a possible structural model of the as-prepared C-dots, energy level structures responsible for the red luminescence and the corresponding energy transfer quenching mechanism for Cu2+ detection using C-dot solution were proposed, as schematically shown in Fig. 7 a and b. After solvothermal reaction, N-rich C-dots were formed through the dehydrolysis/carbonization/nitridation processes with the addition urea as nitrogen source. The formation of pyrrolic N-related defects on the surface of C-dots will induce extra energy states in the gap between HOMO and LUMO states originated from CC bonds of sp2 C. The red luminescence yields when electrons in the N-related states radiatively relax to the HOMO one. After introducing Cu2+ ions into C-dot solution, they will adhere to the surface of C-dots owing to the strong interactions between the N functional groups and Cu2+ ions. Benefited from their short separation, efficient energy transfer from N-related states to Cu2+ quenching centers will induce significant decrement for the selective red luminescence of C-dots. Notably, related to yellow one, the significant weakening of red emission is observed probably owing to stronger interactions between the N-related groups and Cu2+ ions than those between the O-related ones and Cu2+ ions [6] as well as more suitable energy-state matching between N-related emitting-states and Cu2+ quenching ones. Finally, red PL decay curves of C-dot solutions upon the addition of diverse metal ions are shown in Fig. 7c. Remarkable decrease of lifetime for C-dots was observed only when Cu2+ ions were introduced into the solution, confirming the selective sensing of Cu2+. With increase of Cu2+ concentration, the gradual decrease of lifetime (Fig. 7d) supports the above-mentioned energy transfer mechanism.

In summary, we have developed a facile solvothermal reaction route for the synthesis of ultrasmall and monodisperse multi-color fluorescent C-dots. The quantum yields of blue, yellow and red emissions reached as high as 41%, 43% and 39%, respectively. Impressively, the C-dot solution exhibited wavelength-selective Cu2+ sensing with severe quenching of red luminescence and was sensitive to the detection of Cu2+ ions in the nanomolar concentration (LOD = 40 nM). Additionally, the Cu2+ detecting process is reversible and can be carried out for several times without significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity. The formation of pyrrolic N-related emitting states on the surface of C-dots was proposed to be responsible for the red luminescence and the strong interactions between the N functional groups and Cu2+ ions was believed to contribute to the ultrasensitive Cu2+ sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LR15E020001), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51572065 and 61372025) and 151 talent's projects in the second level of Zhejiang Province.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Maximum contaminant level goals and national primary drinking water regulations for lead and copper: Final rule
